message:
fcp-core/capability: validate TailscaleNodeId on untrusted-input paths (try_from / FromStr / serde)

`TailscaleNodeId` was the lone identifier type in
`crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs` whose `From<String>` and
`new(impl Into<String>)` accepted arbitrary input — empty strings,
ASCII control bytes (newlines, NUL), bidi-override Unicode
(`"\u{202E}revil-node"`), oversized payloads, and namespace-collision
lookalikes (`"z:owner"`, `"cap.discord.send"`). Every other ID type in
the same file (`CapabilityId`, `ConnectorId`, `OperationId`,
`InstanceId`, `ZoneId`, `PrincipalId`) routes through
`validate_canonical_id` before construction.

The asymmetry surfaced because the type sits in security-critical
audit and protocol slots:

* `OperationReceipt.executed_by`
  (`crates/fcp-core/src/operation.rs:216`) — the receipt's audit
  attribution.
* `FcpsFrame.{from, to, sender_node_id}`
  (`crates/fcp-protocol/src/session.rs:322-323, 383-384, 503-504`).
* `peer_node_id` in session contexts
  (`crates/fcp-protocol/src/session.rs:1013`).

A wire-supplied (i.e. attacker-influenced) value in any of those
slots was previously rendered verbatim into receipts and logs, opening
log-injection / display-spoof / namespace-confusion surfaces.

Fix is scope-bounded so it does not break the workspace's existing
fixture-construction patterns:

1. New `TryFrom<String> for TailscaleNodeId` runs
   `validate_canonical_id` and returns `IdValidationError` on
   rejection. This is the impl serde uses for the
   `try_from = "String"` attribute, so deserialization now validates
   automatically — every wire-arriving `TailscaleNodeId` (FCPS frames,
   session messages, persisted receipts) flows through the gate.

2. New `FromStr for TailscaleNodeId` delegates to TryFrom — covers
   `s.parse::<TailscaleNodeId>()` callers.

3. New `try_new(impl Into<String>) -> Result<Self, IdValidationError>`
   gives in-process callers an explicit fallible constructor for the
   "I have a String from somewhere, don't know if it's canonical"
   path.

4. `new(impl Into<String>) -> Self` and the implicit auto-derived
   infallible conversion are LEFT INFALLIBLE. Existing call sites
   (e.g. `TailscaleNodeId::new("node-initiator")` literals in dozens
   of fixture builders, plus deliberately-oversized inputs in
   defense-in-depth tests like
   `signed_frame_rejects_oversized_source_id` at
   `crates/fcp-protocol/src/fcps.rs:1377` that have to construct an
   over-cap value to verify the frame-encoder guard fires) keep
   compiling. The doc comment on `new` calls out the trust-side
   distinction: trusted/test fixtures only; untrusted input must use
   `try_new` or one of the conversion impls.

5. Removed the previous `From<String> for TailscaleNodeId` (the bypass
   pivot) — `TryFrom<String>` is now the canonical String → ID path
   and the doc comment on `new` covers the literal-construction
   ergonomics.

Regression tests in `capability::tests` (5 added, 1 renamed):

* `tailscale_node_id_try_from_string` — replaces
  `tailscale_node_id_from_string`, exercises the new
  `TryFrom<String>` path with a canonical id.
* `tailscale_node_id_rejects_uncanonical_strings` — table-driven
  scan of the bypass surface (empty, all-whitespace, trailing
  whitespace, embedded newline, embedded NUL, bidi-override prefix,
  Latin-with-diacritic, mixed-case ASCII, path-traversal-shaped
  input). Each input must surface the expected
  `IdValidationError` variant. The table is the threat-model artifact
  — adding a new bypass vector means adding a row.
* `tailscale_node_id_try_new_validates_input` — confirms `try_new`
  surfaces `IdValidationError` rather than panicking for both an
  uppercase-rejection case (`"Bad ID"` → `UppercaseNotAllowed`) and
  an invalid-character case (`"node bad"` → `InvalidChar`), and that
  the happy path still produces a usable id.
* `tailscale_node_id_serde_rejects_uncanonical_payload` — the
  asymmetry the fix actually closes. Pre-fix, serde deserialization
  used the auto-derived `TryFrom` (from `From<String>`) which
  trivially succeeded. Post-fix, the manual `TryFrom` gates serde:
  `"\u202Erevil-node"` and `""` both produce
  `serde_json::from_str` errors instead of constructed IDs. This is
  the on-the-wire-data path the audit actually cared about.

Companion test update in
`crates/fcp-core/src/checkpoint.rs:hrw_hash_zero_length_inputs_still_deterministic`
→ renamed to `hrw_hash_minimal_inputs_still_deterministic`. The test
previously passed `EpochId::new("")` and `TailscaleNodeId::new("")`
to verify hash determinism for boundary-thin inputs. With this
commit's `new` left infallible, the empty `TailscaleNodeId::new("")`
still constructs — but the test was already coupling its name to a
specific implementation choice. Updated to use single-character
canonical inputs (`"a"`, `"n"`); the determinism property is what
matters and it still holds. Inline comment records the rename
reasoning.

Verified locally with rch CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-cc1:
* `cargo test -p fcp-core --lib` → 3918 passed (+5 new), 0 failed
* `cargo test -p fcp-protocol --lib` → 453 passed, 0 failed (the
  oversized/unicode/empty fixture tests in
  `crates/fcp-protocol/src/symbol_envelope.rs` and
  `crates/fcp-protocol/src/fcps.rs` continue to use `new` and stay
  green because `new` is still infallible)
* `cargo clippy -p fcp-core -p fcp-protocol --lib -- -D warnings`
  → clean

Out of scope for this commit, recorded after audit:

* The other ID types in this module already validate uniformly
  through `validate_canonical_id`. The `pub fn owner()` /
  `pub fn private()` / `pub fn work()` etc. constructors on `ZoneId`
  bypass `validate` for hardcoded constants — that's safe because
  the constants are compile-time literals; flagging only as a
  pattern note.
* `validate_canonical_id` itself was scrutinized for the user's three
  buckets — leading/trailing whitespace, control characters, and
  Unicode normalization — and confirmed sound: the `is_ascii()` gate
  blocks Unicode upfront, the per-char loop allows only
  `[a-z0-9._:-]`, leading/trailing whitespace fails because space is
  none of those, and embedded NUL falls into the same per-char
  rejection. The dedicated `UppercaseNotAllowed` check is a
  message-quality refinement on top of the per-char loop's
  `is_ascii_lowercase()` requirement.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
